name: Lake
overview: Lake is a small hamlet on the west bank of the Hampshire Avon in the Woodford Valley, about 2.5 miles south of Amesbury and roughly 7-8 miles north of Salisbury in Wiltshire. With Wilsford and Normanton it forms the civil parish of Wilsford cum Lake (parish population 191 at the 2011 census), which sits within the Stonehenge World Heritage Site and is studded with Bronze Age round barrows. The hamlet is best known for Lake House, a chequerboard flint-and-stone manor restored by the architect Detmar Blow around 1910 and long associated with the musician Sting and Trudie Styler, whose 16th-century manor stands nearby. It is a linear riverside settlement with no shop or pub of its own; everyday needs are met in Amesbury and Salisbury.
character: A tiny, tranquil and very private riverside hamlet of period and listed houses set among water meadows, downland and prehistoric barrows. The mood is rural, affluent and quiet, with no through traffic and footpaths and cycle routes (the Wiltshire Cycleway) running close by.
amenities: No shop or pub in the hamlet; nearest day-to-day services in Amesbury (~2.5 mi)
Full shopping, supermarkets and amenities in Salisbury (~7-8 mi)
St Michael's parish church at Wilsford (rebuilt 1852)
Woodford Valley village hall and community facilities nearby
Footpaths and the Wiltshire Cycleway through the valley

commutes.notes: Via the Woodford Valley road / A345
From Salisbury station (SWR), drive ~20 min to the station

prices.summary: Lake itself rarely trades and is dominated by large period and listed houses; the wider SP3 / Woodford Valley market is firmly in the premium bracket, with detached country houses typically well into seven figures and very limited supply.

thingsToDo: Walking and cycling the Woodford Valley and Wiltshire Cycleway
Exploring the Lake barrow group and Stonehenge World Heritage landscape
Stonehenge (a short drive north via Amesbury)
Riverside and downland footpaths along the Hampshire Avon
placesToEat: The Bridge Inn, Upper Woodford (riverside pub)
Wide choice of restaurants and cafes in Salisbury (~7-8 mi)
pros: Exceptionally quiet, private riverside setting in the Woodford Valley
Inside the Stonehenge World Heritage Site with rich archaeology and walks
Good local primary and easy reach of Salisbury's grammar and independent schools
Salisbury main line (London Waterloo) within a 20-minute drive
cons: No shop, pub or school within the hamlet itself; car essential
Very limited and expensive housing stock, dominated by large period houses
Heritage and conservation constraints limit alteration and new build
Seasonal visitor traffic in the wider Stonehenge area
whoItSuits: Buyers seeking a quiet, prestigious riverside village with strong heritage and good schools within reach, who don't need amenities on the doorstep and can budget well above the regional average.
nearestStation: Salisbury (~6-7 miles, SWR mainline); Grateley (~8 miles)
